metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to '13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE' to match the resolved count in the body and eliminate the title/body conflict.
findings.finding: The plan title says '1 MISSING_CREATE' but the body (RESOLVED section and AC1) states '4 MISSING_CREATE' after further write activity. An executor reading only the title, or using the title as the target count, would stop at 1 and miss 3 real faults.
findings.location: Plan title vs. RESOLVED section / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Re-audit live-store fsck once ENQUEUE_ENRICH-era code lands: 13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE reported by main's binary against a newer store'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'A3-safe' inline in AC3 (e.g. 'safe under the A3 data-integrity policy: no full-log rebuild, no in-place mutation of tracked event files') so an executor can evaluate compliance without external context.
findings.finding: The term 'A3-safe tooling' in AC3 is unexplained jargon with no definition in the plan. An executor who does not know what 'A3' refers to cannot independently determine whether a given tool or approach satisfies this constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the 'no ticket's status/assignee changed' condition in AC3 — for example: capture `rebar show <ticket-id>` output for all 13 affected tickets before and after remediation and diff them, or run `rebar fsck --check-status` if available, to produce a concrete before/after artifact.
findings.finding: AC3's verification condition 'with no ticket's status/assignee changed' has no stated proving command. The executor is left to determine unspecified how to verify that status and assignee values are unchanged after retiring 13 files and deleting 4 dirs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-remediation step: `for id in <list-of-13-ticket-ids>; do rebar show $id >> pre-remediation-status.txt; done` and a post-remediation diff: `for id in <list-of-13-ticket-ids>; do rebar show $id >> post-remediation-status.txt; done && diff pre-remediation-status.txt post-remediation-status.txt`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (and/or the Context opening sentence) to reflect the final resolved counts — 13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE — eliminating the contradiction with the RESOLVED section and AC1.
findings.finding: The plan title and opening Context line report '13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 1 MISSING_CREATE' (implying 14 total), while the RESOLVED section and AC1's DONE annotation both state '13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE' (17 total). This is a genuine cross-section contradiction that could cause an auditor or future reader to trust the wrong count.
findings.location: Title / Context paragraph 1 vs. RESOLVED section and AC1 DONE annotation
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'Re-audit live-store fsck: 13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE confirmed real-but-benign, remediation pending' and update the first Context sentence to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out step for the phantom-dir deletion — e.g., `cp -r` or `tar` snapshot of the 4 dirs to a temp location before deletion — so the operation is recoverable if the safety justification proves incorrect.
findings.finding: AC3 prescribes deleting 4 local phantom dirs as part of remediation, which is an irreversible destructive operation. The plan justifies the deletion (0 tracked files, not in HEAD, no structural refs) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out path, violating T4's requirement that acknowledgement alone is insufficient — a reversibility mechanism must be specified.
findings.location: AC3 — remediation step: 'delete the 4 local phantom dirs'
findings.suggested_fix: Before the delete step, add: 'Snapshot each phantom dir: `tar czf /tmp/phantom-dirs-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz <dir1> <dir2> <dir3> <dir4>`. Retain for 7 days; delete the backup only after confirming fsck is clean and no downstream process references the dirs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single owning ticket (this ticket or 115b) for the store-mutation step and specify a quiesce window or ordering constraint (e.g., pause the reconciler or confirm no in-flight writes) before executing the rename/delete sequence.
findings.finding: The remediation is designated 'shared remediation tracked jointly with 115b' but no single ticket is named as the owner of the store-mutation step, so both tickets could independently attempt to rename/delete the same files in the live store concurrently, creating a TOCTOU window; additionally, no guard is specified against live reconciler writes interleaving with the rename sequence during remediation.
findings.location: AC3 / Notes — 'shared remediation tracked jointly with 115b'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Remediation ownership' note: '115b owns the physical rename/delete; this ticket owns pre- and post-verification fsck runs. Before the rename sequence, confirm the reconciler is idle (no pending Jira webhook queue) to close the window for new orphan creation during the operation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the ticket title to reflect the final resolved counts (13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE) so the title matches the documented outcome.
findings.finding: The ticket title states '1 MISSING_CREATE' but the resolved body documents 4 MISSING_CREATE; a reader referencing only the title will have an inaccurate count of the faults addressed, potentially causing confusion in audit trails or when the ticket is linked from other work.
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Re-audit live-store fsck once ENQUEUE_ENRICH-era code lands: 13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE — classified real-but-benign, remediated to zero'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the title and Context paragraph to read '4 MISSING_CREATE' (not '1') so the scope of phantom-dir deletions is unambiguous to any executor.
findings.finding: The plan title and opening Context paragraph state '1 MISSING_CREATE' but the RESOLVED section, AC1 DONE annotation, and the 115b partner ticket all record '4 MISSING_CREATE' (phantom dirs 957d, 9fa4, cf5d, 110e). An executor scoping the phantom-dir deletion step from the title alone would delete only one dir instead of four.
findings.location: Title ('13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 1 MISSING_CREATE') and Context paragraph ('14 issues … MISSING_CREATE + ORPHAN_EVENT')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'Re-audit live-store fsck once ENQUEUE_ENRICH-era code lands: 13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE reported by main's binary against a newer store'. Update the Context paragraph's '14 issues' to '17 issues (13 ORPHAN_EVENT + 4 MISSING_CREATE after write activity)' to match the RESOLVED section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step in AC3 to commit and push the .json.retired renames and phantom-dir deletions to the shared branch (e.g., `git add -A && git commit -m 'chore: retire orphan EDIT events + remove phantom dirs (8c4f/115b remediation)' && git push`) so the remediation persists across all clones.
findings.finding: The plan specifies renaming orphan .json files to .json.retired and deleting phantom dirs, but does not specify committing and pushing these mutations to the shared git branch. Because the store is a git-tracked append-only log, without a git commit + push (or equivalent reconciler-commit step), other clones will retain the original .json files on their next pull, causing the ORPHAN_EVENT faults to resurface—the remediation will not persist across the distributed store.
findings.location: AC3 — remediation step: 'retire each orphan EDIT file (.json -> .json.retired, which fsck skips) and delete the 4 local phantom dirs, then confirm rebar fsck reports zero'
findings.suggested_fix: After the retire renames and phantom-dir deletions, run: `git -C <tracker> add -A && git -C <tracker> commit --no-verify -m 'chore: retire orphan EDIT events + delete phantom dirs (fsck 8c4f/115b remediation)' && git -C <tracker> push`. Add this as an explicit sub-step in AC3 between the rename/delete operations and the final fsck confirmation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a code citation or a dry-run test) that the current `rebar fsck` binary skips `.json.retired` files before committing to this as the remediation mechanism for all 13 ORPHAN_EVENT faults.
findings.finding: The plan's entire AC3 remediation strategy pivots on the assertion that `rebar fsck` silently skips files with a `.json.retired` extension, but this capability is stated as fact with no citation to code, documentation, or prior observed behaviour. If the tool does not implement this skip, renaming the 13 orphan files will not reduce the ORPHAN_EVENT count to zero, and the AC3 acceptance gate ('confirm `rebar fsck` reports zero ORPHAN_EVENT') will silently fail.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('retire each orphan EDIT file (.json -> .json.retired, which fsck skips)')
findings.suggested_fix: Search the fsck source for the `.retired` skip (e.g. `grep -r 'retired' src/` in the rebar repo) and paste the relevant code path or test into a comment on this ticket; if the feature is absent, add a small fsck patch (or use an alternative like a dedicated fsck ignore-list flag) and track that work explicitly here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-deletion backup step (archive or copy-out) for the 4 phantom dirs and assert the expected store state after a partial failure or rollback (per T11-e POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE requirement). Note `DATA LOSS RISK` explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan calls for deleting the 4 phantom directories but provides no back-out path and does not assert the store state after a partial failure or rollback. Directory deletion is irreversible without a prior backup, yet no backup or compensating step (e.g., `cp -r` / archive before delete) is specified. The plan also does not describe what the store looks like if the process crashes mid-deletion (e.g., 2 of 4 dirs removed).
findings.location: AC3 / Notes — 'delete the 4 local phantom dirs'
findings.suggested_fix: Before any deletion, run `tar -czf phantom-dirs-backup-$(date +%s).tar.gz <dir1> <dir2> <dir3> <dir4>` and record the archive path in the ticket. Document the post-partial-failure state: 'if the script exits mid-run, only the dirs listed in the script output as deleted are gone; re-run with the backup available for restore.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the reconciler (and any compaction process) must be paused or fenced during the remediation window, and document the procedure for safely draining in-flight writes before retiring orphan files and deleting phantom dirs.
findings.finding: The root cause (compaction-vs-reconciler write race) is explicitly left unresolved in this ticket, yet the plan does not address whether the reconciler must be quiesced during remediation. If the reconciler writes a new EDIT to a file being retired, or recreates a phantom dir, during the remediation window, the remediation may be immediately invalidated or leave the store in an inconsistent state.
findings.location: Notes — 'Root cause of the recurring orphan race … is a separate durable-fix follow-up, out of scope'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs are plausibly 'false positives from binary/store version skew (the older binary cannot interpret the newer events' effects, so replay bookkeeping looks orphaned)' — but the fsck ORPHAN_EVENT check at fsck.py:313 already explicitly skips any event whose type is not in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES. ENQUEUE_ENRICH is in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (not KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES) at v0.7.1, so a v0.7.1 binary would already NOT flag ENQUEUE_ENRICH files as ORPHAN_EVENT. The stated false-positive mechanism is not supported by the code: the 13 orphans must be events of types that ARE in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, and version skew from ENQUEUE_ENRICH cannot explain them via the stated path. The plan's AC3 ('note the false-positive mechanism here and close') may record a causally incorrect explanation if the findings clear.
findings.location: Plan body: 'false positives from binary/store version skew (the older binary cannot interpret the newer events' effects, so replay bookkeeping looks orphaned)'; AC item 'If findings clear: note the false-positive mechanism here and close'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'note the false-positive mechanism here' with a more precise frame: first confirm the event types of the 13 orphans (they must be in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES to appear as ORPHAN_EVENT); then trace whether a compaction raced with the new write session, capturing a snapshot that pre-dated the orphaned KNOWN-type events, rather than attributing the orphans to ENQUEUE_ENRICH version skew.

findings.finding: The plan's hypothesis (a) — that ENQUEUE_ENRICH version-skew is the likely cause of the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs — is mechanically incomplete. The ORPHAN_EVENT check in fsck.py:313 already skips any event type NOT in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, meaning unknown/newer types like ENQUEUE_ENRICH would be silently passed over, not flagged. The 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs must therefore involve KNOWN_EVENT_TYPE events appearing pre-snapshot, and any ENQUEUE_ENRICH involvement is at most indirect (e.g., concurrent ENQUEUE_ENRICH writes advancing HLC timestamps so a subsequent KNOWN-type event lands pre-snapshot). Without this causal pathway spelled out, AC3 ('note the false-positive mechanism here and close') risks being fulfilled by a superficial explanation — or, worse, genuine KNOWN-type orphans are dismissed as version-skew artifacts.
findings.location: AC3: 'If findings clear: note the false-positive mechanism here and close'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body and AC3, note: 'The ORPHAN_EVENT check (fsck.py:313) already skips non-KNOWN event types, so the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs are on KNOWN-type events. The likely indirect path: ENQUEUE_ENRICH writes from the concurrent session advanced the HLC clock, causing subsequent KNOWN-type events (STATUS/EDIT/etc.) to be written with timestamps that land before a snapshot compacted by the older binary, making them appear as pre-snapshot orphans. If this is confirmed after upgrade, note this mechanism and verify no KNOWN-type events were silently dropped.'

findings.finding: AC1's gating condition ('After the ENQUEUE_ENRICH-introducing change merges to main') appears already satisfied: ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, and DONE_ENRICH are present in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES in the current main binary (_version.py:44-54), so is_unknown_newer_type() already returns False for these types and fsck already exempts them from ORPHAN_EVENT flagging. The plan treats this change as still-pending, which may make AC1's gate stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Verify `ENQUEUE_ENRICH in rebar.reducer._version._NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` on the current main HEAD. If True, restate AC1 as: 'Run `rebar fsck` from the current main binary against the live store and record the counts on this ticket' (no merge prerequisite).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
